title: Spatiotemporal fusion of radar backscatter for enhanced monitoring of surface melt dynamics over Antarctic ice shelves
summary: Surface meltwater over the Antarctic Ice Sheet acts as a primary modulator of the polar energy budget, potentially triggering ice shelf collapse and exerting far-reaching impacts on the global climate system. Radar scatterometers enable daily melt detection but at coarse resolution, whereas Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) achieves fine spatial detail with limited temporal coverage. Spatiotemporal fusion methods present a viable solution to balance this trade-off, but these approaches are applied only to optical remote sensing and have not yet been successfully adapted to radar backscatter data. In this study, we propose the Spatiotemporal Radar Backscatter Data Fusion (SRBDF) method, a multi-source C-band active microwave data fusion technique that can integrate Sentinel-1 and Advanced Scatterometer (ASCAT) observations. SRBDF generates a high-resolution daily backscatter dataset for monitoring surface melt dynamics at the Larsen C and George VI Ice Shelves throughout 2018–2021 melt seasons. The fused dataset preserves spatial details from Sentinel-1, while maintaining the daily frequency of ASCAT. Across the Larsen C and George VI Ice Shelves, SRBDF melt/freeze detection shows overall consistency approaching or above 0.90 with Sentinel-1 and ASCAT. High-resolution daily observations reveal an extreme melt season during 2019–2020, when melt occurrence was more than 50 % above the previous season. Pronounced melt was observed on Larsen C and George VI ice shelves driven by foehn winds and sustained warm air advection from low-speed northerly winds, respectively. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of SRBDF in capturing Antarctic ice shelf surface melt variability and its potential for broader applications in polar surface process research.

title: Mapping the extent, type and remnant areas of the coastal dunes of Australia and New Zealand by integrating multi-source data
summary: Accurate large-scale mapping of coastal dune types is critical for coastal management but remains challenging due to the dunes’ high spatial heterogeneity and the spectral complexity in coastal environments. Therefore, this study aims to develop a coastal dune mapping approach and demonstrate its applicability across Australia and New Zealand. Firstly, we leveraged geological maps to delineate the spatial extent of coastal dune fields, effectively masking out non-dune areas. Secondly, we applied an object-based image analysis (OBIA), followed by a random forest classifier that integrated multi-source features to classify six coastal dune types within the delineated coastal dune fields, including active dunes, foredunes, sandy beaches, semi-stabilized dunes, stabilized dunes, and wetlands (dune slacks). Based on this approach, we generated a 10 m fine classification map of coastal dune types across Australia and New Zealand, covering a total area of 27,564 km 2 . Accuracy assessment yielded an overall classification accuracy of 90.68 %. Most categories achieved satisfactory performance, with stabilized dunes showing the highest accuracy (96.81 %). We also found that approximately 498 km 2 of coastal dune area has been converted to urban development land. However, this development pressure was mitigated by conservation efforts, as 15,781.80 km 2 (59.44 %) of the total coastal dune area was situated within protected areas. This study provides a replicable methodology for large-scale fine classification of coastal dune types. The resulting map offers scientific support for monitoring coastal dune systems state and evolution under human intervention.

title: C2D2: Matching UAV images with cycle-consistency and channel-separation for joint detector and descriptor
summary: Accurate feature matching in Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) imagery is fundamental to earth observation applications. Although jointly learning keypoint detectors and descriptors has become standard practice, existing methods face inherent challenges: keypoint repeatability and descriptor reliability optimize for different visual properties, while patch-based training losses impose locality constraints that limit global geometric consistency. This paper presents C2D2, a network that addresses these limitations through two key innovations. First, a Channel Separation Module (CSM) reduces cross-task interference by enabling task-specific channel emphasis. Second, a cycle consistency loss enforces global geometric constraints beyond local patches, enhancing descriptor robustness with minimal additional training overhead. Extensive evaluation on public benchmarks and a challenging UAV dataset demonstrates improvements on most evaluated metrics: 4.4% higher detector repeatability on HPatches and up to 7.3% improvement in localization recall on the Aachen Day–Night benchmark. These results , achieved with only 0.49M parameters, establish C2D2 as an efficient solution within the sparse feature learning family for demanding remote sensing applications.

title: A SIF-Constrained Noah-MP land surface model for simulating water use efficiency and drought Resilience: Application in a Semi-Arid Basin
summary: Land surface models (LSMs) systematically overestimate evapotranspiration (ET) in water-limited ecosystems due to inadequate representation of plant physiological water stress, undermining reliable water resource projections. We introduce a novel, mechanistic integration of solar-induced fluorescence (SIF) directly into the Ball-Berry stomatal resistance scheme of WRF-Hydro/Noah-MP. In the absence of eddy covariance flux tower measurements within the basin, model evaluation is conducted through inter-comparison with multiple independent gridded reference products (MODIS, GLEAM, GLDAS), and results are interpreted as physically consistent shifts in model behaviour rather than validated improvements in absolute accuracy. The methodology is designed to be transferable, relying only on publicly available SIF products and the widely used Noah–MP model. We developed a self-calibrating SIF factor (0–1) from GOSIF v2 to modulate the net photosynthesis rate, thereby coupling observed photosynthetic activity to stomatal resistance and transpiration. This SIF-enhanced model was applied to the Awash Basin, Ethiopia (2000–2024), and evaluated through inter-comparison with MODIS, GLEAM, and GLDAS. SIF integration increases consistency with the multi–product ensemble: mean annual ET decreased by 36.1 % (from 8.45 to 5.40 mm day⁻ 1 , p < 0.001), thereby reducing the ET discrepancy relative to the ensemble of satellite–based reference products (with α = 0.10 calibrated for this basin). The transpiration fraction of ET increased most during the critical dry season (+7.0 %, p < 0.001), reflecting more realistic water-conservation behaviour. Consequently, Water Use Efficiency (WUE) increased under both normal and drought conditions, and the SIF-constrained Model showed substantially smaller WUE losses during drought than the Control (a 5 % decline versus a 13 % decline, respectively), consistent with a more water-conservative stomatal strategy that reduces transpiration by a greater proportion than photosynthesis under stress. This simulated response aligns with observed plant behaviour in water-limited ecosystems, yielding a drought resilience index of 0.95, compared with 0.87 for the Control. Monthly flux correlations confirmed strong carbon–water coupling (GPP-ET r = 0.93), while annual-scale analysis revealed a developing trade-off in which high-productivity years were least efficient (WUE-ET r = -0.88). Furthermore, unsupervised clustering of SIF-constrained WUE and resilience outputs yielded the first physiologically based agroecological zonation of the basin. We identified four distinct functional zones, including a productive but drought-vulnerable core (21,425 km 2 , WUE = 2.49 g C kg⁻ 1 H 2 O) and resilient but inefficient lowlands (8,175 km 2 , WUE = 0.05 g C kg⁻ 1 H 2 O). This scalable SIF-informed physiological constraint framework reduces a structural LSM bias, yielding more credible ecohydrological projections and providing dynamic, plant-informed targets for climate-resilient land and water planning.

title: A two-stage machine learning framework for Chl-a reconstruction and short-term prediction in eutrophic lakes using GOCI observations
summary: Harmful algal blooms (HABs) in eutrophic lakes threaten aquatic ecosystems and public health, but short-term chlorophyll-a (Chl-a) prediction remains challenging because satellite observations are often discontinuous and bloom dynamics are regulated by interacting environmental and ecological factors. This study developed a two-stage Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) framework for satellite-derived Chl-a reconstruction and short-term prediction in Lake Taihu. The first-stage model (XGBoost_Interp) reconstructed Chl-a on dates without valid GOCI/GOCI-II retrievals and generated a continuous daily Chl-a series for 2011–2024, achieving good performance (R 2 = 0.83, RMSE = 5.72 μg/L, MAPE = 9.16%). Using the reconstructed series and environmental variables, the second-stage model (XGBoost_Pred) predicted subsequent Chl-a with comparable accuracy (R 2 = 0.83, RMSE = 5.62 μg/L, MAPE = 8.97%). However, independent validation from January to March 2025 showed lower accuracy (R 2 = 0.69, RMSE = 9.45 μg/L) and some overestimation, suggesting that further calibration and evaluation are still needed when valid satellite observations are unavailable for extended periods. SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) identified wind speed, temperature, atmospheric pressure, day of year, and antecedent Chl-a as important predictors. After lake-specific recalibration, the framework also performed well in Lake Chaohu, indicating its practical potential for other large eutrophic lakes. Nevertheless, broader application requires further validation with nutrient-related variables, multi-sensor data, high-frequency field observations, and more lakes with different environmental conditions.

title: Reproducible mapping of marine biodiversity using autonomous surface vehicles and deep learning
summary: Monitoring shallow-water marine ecosystems requires precise, low-cost and reproducible methods, yet traditional diver-based surveys are labor-intensive, subject to observer bias and limited in spatial coverage. In this study, we present an autonomous framework that integrates deep learning techniques with open-source data acquisition platforms to overcome these challenges. Our approach uses a transformer-based model to classify different coral morphotypes, seagrass species and benthic habitats. This model is trained on the publicly available Seatizen Atlas image dataset . Data were collected using low-cost autonomous surface vehicles (ASVs) equipped with action cameras, differential GNSS receivers, single-beam echosounders and inertial measurement units. The ASVs provide precise geolocation, depth and attitude measurements, synchronized with image timestamps and embedded as standardized metadata, ensuring interoperability and reproducibility. Beyond image classification, the ASVs enable bathymetric surveys and photogrammetric reconstruction for three-dimensional mapping of surveyed areas. Field validation at Réunion Island and Aldabra Atoll demonstrates that this framework delivers cost-effective, non-invasive and standardized monitoring, while automating laborious annotation tasks and increasing survey scalability. By combining open-source datasets, AI models and versatile ASV functionalities, this methodology supports reproducible ecological assessments and informs conservation decisions.

title: A coherence time-series framework for earthquake-induced building damage detection using SAR
summary: Earthquake-induced building damage detection using interferometric SAR coherence is complicated by multiple decorrelation mechanisms unrelated to structural damage. Most existing approaches rely on coherence changes extracted from a limited number of image pairs, which makes it difficult to isolate structural damage signals from other temporally varying contributions. This study proposes a sequential coherence time-series framework that explicitly models decorrelation components across pre-, co-, and post-seismic phases according to their distinct spatiotemporal characteristics, and derives two complementary damage indicators: the average pre-to-co-seismic coherence ratio (ρ0) and the average pre-to-post-seismic coherence ratio (ρ1). Both indicators combine candidate pixel selection and temporal averaging to suppress damage-irrelevant coherence variability while amplifying step-type transitions associated with building damage. ρ0 supports rapid initial assessment using the first post-event acquisition, whereas ρ1 provides higher specificity to persistent structural damage by excluding the co-seismic pair from the indicator computation. The framework is validated using Sentinel-1 time series over Mashiki Town and Wajima City, affected by the 2016 Kumamoto and 2024 Noto Peninsula earthquakes. Compared with three representative coherence-based damage indicators, the proposed approach consistently reduces false alarms in both urban and non-urban areas. ρ1 demonstrates substantially improved precision–recall performance under class imbalance conditions, with AUPRC values of 0.56 and 0.66 at the two sites, compared to 0.22–0.39 for the reference methods. These results indicate that explicit temporal modeling of coherence decorrelation provides a physically interpretable and operationally practical basis for post-earthquake damage mapping.

title: Can the MODIS EVI formula be directly applied to other sensor data? A case study with Landsat data
summary: The question of whether the MODIS enhanced vegetation index (EVI) can be directly applied to other satellite sensors has remained unexplored for nearly three decades since its development. This is important because the EVI's empirical parameters are specifically designed for MODIS. For a long time, users have applied these MODIS-based parameters directly to other sensor data without modification. Therefore, this study systematically explores this issue using Landsat-8/9 data. We introduce a novel methodological framework: using NDVI (parameter-free) as a reference benchmark and designing an auxiliary index (EVIb) to isolate the impact of the blue band, as it is introduced in EVI. Results show that, based on the synchronized observations of MODIS and Landsat-8/9 across eleven vegetation test areas, the deviation between MODIS and Landsat-8/9 EVI is, on average, 1.7 times larger than that of their NDVI, with an R 2 deviation reaching 10%. Analysis confirms that the blue band is not the primary cause of the deviation; instead, the discrepancy arises from the inapplicability of the MODIS-specific parameters to Landsat-8/9 data. Sensitivity analysis reveals strong interactions among the four parameters of EVI, with the soil adjustment factor (L) being the most sensitive. Based on these findings, the study proposes and tests two parameter optimization strategies for Landsat-8/9 EVI: a site-specific cross-calibration method that reduces the mean absolute percentage bias (|PB|) between the MODIS and Landsat-8/9 EVI from 13% to nearly 0%, and a generalized parameter optimization using the Nelder-Mead algorithm, which reduces the average |PB| to 8.33%. Cross-validation indicates that the optimized parameters can reduce bias by approximately 32.5% in unseen areas from similar biomes. This study concludes that the direct cross-sensor application of MODIS EVI parameters introduces significant bias. We recommend sensor-specific parameter optimization and provide a transferable methodological framework to guide future efforts towards consistent multi-sensor vegetation monitoring.

title: Analyzing dominant driving factors of ground deformation in mining area via multimodal feature fusion and explainable modeling
summary: Ground deformation in areas of natural resource extraction results from the interplay of multiple anthropogenic and hydrogeological factors. Identifying the main factors and their relationship to deformation is crucial for mitigating disasters when extreme events happen. Here, we show how multimodal artificial intelligence models can disentangle the effects of dynamic and static features related to deformation in the Rhineland coalfield in Germany. We systematically investigate the synergistic effects of multi-source data by integrating static environmental features, including mining areas, urban areas, fault lines, farmland, and geological distributions, with dynamic features, such as surface deformation time-series and annual groundwater-level observations, and organising them into 12 nested multimodal subsets. Using mainstream deep learning modules, including Convolutional Neural Networks, Graph Convolutional Networks, and Transformer, we build six distinct network architectures and evaluate them under comparable settings through progressive feature fusion and feature ablation. The results show that multimodal fusion consistently improves predictive accuracy compared to a model driven only by deformation data, indicating that static spatial distribution information enhances surface deformation prediction when incorporated as contextual background. Within the controlled feature-space design, feature ablation provides stable channel-level comparisons, whereas SHAP remains a useful complementary post hoc attribution method. At the regional scale, geology and groundwater emerge as the most important predictors of the overall deformation pattern, which is consistent with the interpretation that geological conditions shape the hydrogeological setting and the spatial expression of groundwater fluctuations. At the local scale, fault information becomes the most important predictor within spatially confined high-impact zones, because structural discontinuities concentrate deformation gradients and groundwater-related responses. This distinction between regional background control and local structural triggering provides a data-driven basis for assessing deformation susceptibility and mitigating hazards in mining areas.

title: Coupling functional heterogeneity activation and distance impedance: a distance-decay model for urban population spatial interactions
summary: Distance-decay models, which formulate the relationship between interaction intensity and distance, are fundamental to understanding population spatial interactions. However, classical models struggle to capture the non-monotonic distance-decay patterns in cities, while statistically driven models lack geographic semantic interpretability for their parameters. Therefore, we propose the Activation-Impedance Decay Model (AIDM). Building upon the distance friction effect, AIDM integrates the driving force of urban functional heterogeneity on spatial interactions. By quantifying this force as an activation process of interaction potential that increases non-linearly with distance, the model accurately reproduces the hump-shaped distribution of spatial interaction. Considering the fractal properties of urban structures and functions, power-law functions are employed to model the non-linear spatial transition gradients of functional heterogeneity and distance friction. In addition, AIDM introduces two parameters with explicit geographical semantics to quantify the characteristic spatial scales of urban population interactions: one represents the local functional homogeneity scale (ranging from hundreds of meters to several kilometers), within which functional similarity suppresses spatial interactions; the other denotes the distance friction scale (on the order of several kilometers), beyond which increasing travel costs rapidly outweigh interaction benefits, thereby significantly reducing interaction intensity. These parameters provide an interpretable quantification of the effective spatial ranges of functional complementarity and distance friction, thereby revealing the underlying spatial mechanisms shaping intra-urban interaction patterns. Experimental results across different spatial scales of diverse cities demonstrate that AIDM consistently outperforms baseline models, achieving an AIC reduction of over 29.9%. Correlation analyses with urban functional and structural characteristics further validate the AIDM parameters' geographical semantics. In empirical applications, population spatial accessibility derived from AIDM aligns closely with observed urban vitality distribution. Furthermore, the model effectively differentiates variations in interaction intensities and patterns across distinct functional units. Thus, AIDM provides an interpretable analytical framework for uncovering the regularities of complex intra-urban population spatial interactions.
